does: This paper develops a systematic framework for AI risk modeling that integrates scenario building—causal mapping from hazards to potential harms—with quantitative risk estimation of likelihood and severity. It reviews and adapts classical risk analysis techniques including Fault Tree Analysis, Event Tree Analysis, FMEA/FMECA, STPA, and Bayesian networks to the AI context. The paper surveys current academic and industry approaches to AI risk management, identifying fragmentation across capability benchmarks, safety cases, and isolated quantitative studies. It empirically examines how mature risk-management practices operate across nuclear energy, aviation, cybersecurity, financial systems, and submarine operations, identifying a common pattern: these domains combine deterministic safety guarantees for unacceptable failure modes with probabilistic risk assessment across broader operational scenarios. The paper argues this dual approach should inform advanced-AI governance infrastructure.
connects: This work builds on a long history of engineering risk analysis methods developed for high-stakes industrial domains, particularly the formal frameworks pioneered in nuclear safety, aviation, and other critical infrastructure sectors. It responds to recent AI safety research that has produced important safety case studies and capability benchmarks but often in isolation from each other. The paper connects to growing academic work on formal methods for AI safety, value alignment, and specification problems, while also engaging with practical industry initiatives in AI governance and testing. It synthesizes insights from interdisciplinary risk management literature and applies them to the emerging challenge of advanced AI systems, bridging the gap between domain-specific safety engineering practices and the novel characteristics of AI risk landscapes.
enables: This framework enables the development of more comprehensive risk-management infrastructure for advanced AI systems by providing a systematic methodology for integrating diverse safety approaches. It points toward future work in formalizing causal pathways from AI capabilities to real-world harms, developing probabilistic models of advanced-AI failure modes, and establishing governance standards that combine AI capability constraints with broader risk monitoring. The work supports creation of industry standards and regulatory approaches for AI risk management, similar to those developed for nuclear and aviation sectors. It enables comparative analysis of different AI safety techniques within a unified framework and suggests priorities for research into quantifying low-probability, high-impact AI scenarios. The framework also enables more informed decision-making about resource allocation between deterministic safety guarantees and probabilistic risk mitigation strategies in AI development.
